summary:
The Wall Street Crash of 1929, also known as Black Tuesday, was a catastrophic stock market collapse that began on 24 October 1929 and accelerated dramatically on 28–29 October, wiping out billions of dollars in market value in a matter of days. The crash followed a decade of speculative excess during the Roaring Twenties, when stock prices had soared far beyond any justification from underlying corporate earnings. Easy credit, margin trading (buying stocks with borrowed money), and widespread speculative enthusiasm had inflated a bubble that the market's sudden loss of confidence burst with devastating force.

On Black Thursday (24 October), panicked selling sent prices plummeting; a consortium of major banks temporarily stabilized the market by purchasing large blocks of stock. But the panic resumed on Black Monday and Black Tuesday, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average losing nearly a quarter of its value in two days. Investors who had purchased stocks on margin faced devastating losses; many banks and brokerage firms were wiped out. The psychological shock to consumer and business confidence was severe and lasting.

The crash was the trigger but not the sole cause of the [[event:2650|Great Depression]]. The subsequent collapse of thousands of banks — partly caused by their exposure to the stock market — contracted the money supply and froze credit, turning a financial crisis into a full economic collapse. The crash exposed the speculative character of 1920s prosperity and the inadequacy of existing financial regulation. In the United States, the Glass-Steagall Act (1933) subsequently separated commercial and investment banking, a regulatory wall that remained in place for decades as a direct lesson from 1929.
